(Article 1/3) | by Vincent Meindl | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@meivin_tech/what-is-vpc-service-controls-and-why-should-you-care-article-1-3-813dde56b8df"},{"title":"How to Configure Egress Rules for VPC Service Controls Perimeters","url":"https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-17-how-to-configure-egress-rules-for-vpc-service-controls-perimeters/view"},{"title":"Ingress and egress rules | VPC Service Controls | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/ingress-egress-rules"}]

The search has returned relevant results about VPC Service Controls, workforce identity federation, and STS egress rules. Here are the key findings:

## Workforce Identity Federation with VPC Service Controls

For Security Token Service API requests where the audience is a Workforce Identity Federation pool (which is an organization-level resource), you must configure egress rules. This is necessary because VPC Service Controls does not support adding organization-level resources directly to perimeters.

## Egress Rule Configuration

The following egress rules allow requests originating from the perimeter to reach the Workforce STS APIs that interact with organization-level Workforce pools: egressTo with serviceName 'sts.googleapis.com', methodSelectors with method '*', resources set to '*', and egressFrom with identityType ANY_IDENTITY.

## Identity Configuration for Token Exchanges

When you create an ingress or egress rule to allow token exchanges, you must set the identity type to ANY_IDENTITY because the token method has no authorization.

## Third-Party Identities Support

VPC Service Controls supports third-party identities such as workforce pool users and workload identities in ingress and egress rules.

The documentation indicates that when using Workforce Identity Federation with VPC Service Controls perimeters, egress rules are the key mechanism to allow STS API calls to organization-level workforce pools, since those resources cannot be directly added to perimeters at the organization level.

attachment.snippet:
1	# Claude Code Project Memory
2	
3	## Directory Permissions
4	Full read and edit access is pre-approved for all files in:
5	- `Tools/`
6	- `Memos/`
7	- `../cnmp_CellNodeMessagePrototype/lenses/`
8	
9	## File Acronym Mappings
10	
11	Per-kit acronym mappings live in each kit's context file (loaded via `@` includes below).
12	- RBK: `@Tools/rbk/claude-rbk-acronyms.md`
13	- BUK: `@Tools/buk/claude-buk-core.md`
14	- CMK: `@Tools/cmk/claude-cmk-core.md`
15	- JJK: `@Tools/jjk/claude-jjk-core.md` (+ veiled `@Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/claude-jjk-bhyslop.md`)
16	- VOK: `@Tools/vok/claude-vok-context.md`
17	- GAD: `Tools/gad/CLAUDE.md` (not `@`-included — loaded only when working in that kit)
18	
19	### CNMP Lenses Directory (`../cnmp_CellNodeMessagePrototype/lenses/`)
20	- **ANCIENT** → `a-roe-ANCIENT.md`
21	- **ANNEAL**  → `a-roe-ANNEAL-spec-fine.adoc`
22	- **CRAFT**   → `a-roe-CRAFT-cmodel-format.adoc`
23	- **METAL**   → `a-roe-METAL-sequences.adoc`
24	- **MIND**    → `a-roe-MIND-cmodel-semantic.adoc`
25	- **JRR**     → `jrr-JobRookRadar-sspec.adoc`
26	- **MBC**     → `lens-mbc-MakefileBashConsole-cmodel.adoc`
27	- **YAK**     → `lens-yak-YetAnotherKludge-cmodel.adoc`
28	- **M2C**     → `mcm-M2C-ModelToClaudex.md`
29	- **SRFC**    → `srf-SRFC-StudyRaftConcepts.adoc`
30	- **ABG**     → `wrs-ABG-AccordBuilderGuide.md`
31	- **ALTL**    → `wrs-ALTL-AccordLogicalTaskLens.claudex`
32	- **PMTL**    → `wrs-PMTL-ProtocolMachineryTaskLens.claudex`
33	- **SDTL**    → `wrs-SDTL-ShapeDesignTaskLens.claudex`
34	- **TITL**    → `wrs-TITL-TestInfrastructureTaskLens.claudex`
35	- **TLG**     → `wrs-TLG-TaskLensGuide.md`
36	- **WRC**     → `wrs-WRC-WardRealmConcepts.adoc`
37	- **WCC**     → `WCC-WebClaudetoClaudeCode.md`
38	
39	## Working Preferences
40	- When user mentions an acronym, immediately navigate to the corresponding file
41	- Assume full edit permissions for all files in the three main directories
42	- For bash scripts, prefer functional programming style with clear error handling
43	- For .adoc files, maintain consistent AsciiDoc formatting
44	- For .claudex files, preserve the specific format requirements
45	
46	### Heredoc Delimiter Selection
47	
48	When generating heredocs for stdin content, the delimiter must not appear alone on any line within the content.
49	
50	- **Check content first**: If content includes `EOF` (e.g., code examples showing heredoc patterns), use a different delimiter
51	- **Safe alternatives**: `SPEC`, `CONTENT`, `DOC`, `PACESPEC`, `SLASHCMD`
52	- **Pattern**: `cat <<'DELIM' | command` (quoted delimiter prevents variable expansion)
53	
54	### AsciiDoc Linked Terms
55	When working with .adoc files using MCM patterns:
56	- **Linked Term**: Concept with three parts:
57	  - Attribute reference: `:prefix_snake_case:` (mapping section)
58	  - Replacement text: `<<anchor,Display Text>>` (what readers see)
59	  - Definition: `[[anchor]] {attribute}:: Definition text` (meaning)
60	- Definitions may be grouped in lists or dispersed through document
61	- Maintain consistent prefix categories (e.g., `mcm_`, `rbw_`, `gad_`)
62	- Use snake_case for anchors, match attribute to anchor
63	
64	### Rust Build Discipline
65	
66	Two Rust build targets. Always use the tabtarget, never raw cargo commands.
67	
68	**VOW pipeline** (vvk/jjk/cmk kits — parceled for delivery):
69	- `tt/vow-b.Build.sh` — build vvr binary and install to VVK bin
70	- `tt/vow-t.Test.sh` — run all kit crate tests
71	- `tt/vvw-r.RunVVX.sh <cmd>` — run vvx binary with arguments
72	
73	**Theurge** (rbk's own test infrastructure — dispatches through the unified rbw workbench):
74	- `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` — build theurge crate
75	- `tt/rbw-tt.Test.sh` — run theurge unit tests
76	
77	### Test Execution
78	
79	**Test suites** group fixtures by dependency tier. Composition is owned by
80	`RBTDRC_SUITES` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs`); this table summarizes it.
81	Run the broadest applicable suite:
82	
83	| Suite | Tabtarget | Dependencies | What it covers |
84	|-------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
85	| `fast` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` | None | 10 fixtures: enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke, podvm-resolve, handbook-render, dockerfile-hygiene, foundry-path, recipe-validation, cupel, conformance |
86	| `service` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.service.sh` | GCP credentials | fast + access-probe, hallmark-lifecycle, lode-lifecycle, reliquary-lifecycle, wsl-lifecycle, podvm-lifecycle, batch-vouch (17 fixtures) |
87	| `crucible` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.crucible.sh` | Container runtime | fast + tadmor, srjcl, pluml (13 fixtures) |
88	| `complete` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh` | All of the above | service ∪ crucible (20 fixtures) |
89	
90	**Release/probe suites** — ladders distinguished by project-churn × crucible ×
91	network posture, not dependency tier:
92	
93	| Suite | Tabtarget | Precondition | What it covers |
94	|-------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
95	| `gauntlet` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh` | None (levies fresh projects) | Release-qualification ladder: marshal-zero state → canonical-establish → onboarding-sequence → fast fixtures → crucibles |
96	| `skirmish` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish.sh` | Canonical depot already levied | Mini-gauntlet: depot→build→crucible chain without project churn |
97	| `dogfight` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` | Canonical depot already levied | Cloud-build viability probe: ordain → summon → run, no crucible |
98	| `siege` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.siege.sh` | None (fully local) | Tadmor self-contained: kludge both vessels + security cases |
99	| `blockade` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.blockade.sh` | Depot levied + moriah hallmark ordained | Airgap moriah crucible with credential self-heal |
100	
101	**After code changes**, run the appropriate tier:
102	- Regime/validation changes → `fast`
103	- Foundry/credential changes → `service`
104	- Bottle/sentry/network changes → `crucible`
105	- Pre-release or decomposition sweep → `complete`
106	
107	**Single fixture**: `tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh <name>` (e.g., `tadmor`, `enrollment-validation`, `regime-smoke`)
108	
109	**Single case**: `tt/rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh <fixture> [case-name]` — run one case against an already-charged crucible (no charge/quench). Omit case name to list all cases for the fixture; omit fixture to list all fixtures. Workflow for crucible debugging: charge via `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.{nameplate}.sh`, run individual cases, quench via `tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.{nameplate}.sh` when done.
110	
111	**BUK self-test**: `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` — exercises BUK test framework (kick-tires + bure-tweak, 9 cases)
112	
113	**Sequential only**: Never run fixtures in parallel — they share regime state and container namespaces.
114	
115	## Prefix Naming Discipline ("mint")
116	
117	When asked to "mint" names, apply these rules. Full study: `Memos/memo-20260110-acronym-selection-study.md`
118	
119	### Two Universes
120	
121	**Primary Universe** — ANY persistent identifier, regardless of where it lives. This includes the obvious (code, docs, functions, variables, directories) AND the easy-to-miss: git refs, slash commands, environment variables, paths in target repos, configuration keys. **If it's a name that persists, it's in scope.** Prefixes must be globally unique and respect terminal exclusivity.
122	
123	**Tabtarget Universe** — launchers in `tt/`. These are *colophons* referencing the primary universe. `rbw-` points to the `rbw` workbench; it doesn't consume new prefix space.
124	
125	### Core Rules
126	
127	**Rule 1 - Project Prefix**: Names start with 2-4 char project ID:
128	`rb` (Recipe Bottle), `gad` (GAD), `bu` (BUK), `jj` (Job Jockey), `pb` (Paneboard), `mcm`/`axl` (CMK), `crg`, `wrs`, `hm` (HMK), `lmci`, `vsl` (VSLK)
129	
130	**Rule 2 - Terminal Exclusivity**: A prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both.
131	- `rbg` has children (`rbga`, `rbgb`...) → `rbg` cannot name a thing
132	- `rbi` names Image module → `rbia`, `rbib` forbidden
133	
134	### Primary Universe Patterns
135	
136	| Domain | Pattern | Example |
137	|--------|---------|---------|
138	| Code files | `prefix_Word.ext` | `rbga_ArtifactRegistry.sh` |

... [188 lines truncated] ...

20	
21	| Term | Prefix | Meaning |
22	|------|--------|---------|
23	| **Quoin** | `mcm_` | A formal identifier with full cataloguing: attribute reference in the mapping section, anchor at the definition site, and definition text. The cornerstone — addressable from anywhere, tracked across the constellation. |
24	| **Mapping Section** | `mcm_` | The block of attribute references at the top of a document, between `tag::mapping-section[]` markers. Where quoins are registered. |
25	| **Concept Model** | `mcm_` | A unified constellation of linked terms whose relationships give meaning to a domain. Manifested as an AsciiDoc document following MCM patterns. |
26	| **Category** | `mcm_` | The prefix group (e.g., `bzsn_`, `bzsdp_`, `mcm_`) that scopes a set of related quoins. |
27	| **Variant** | `mcm_` | Suffix modifiers on attribute references: `_s` plural, `_p` possessive, `_ed` past, `_ing` progressive. |
28	| **Annotation** | `mcm_` | An AsciiDoc comment line carrying structured metadata as space-separated prefix terms. Position determines role: primary motif first, dimensions following; the voices relationship is implicit. Example: `//axk_premise`. Legacy `//axl_voices …` form is read-only — never author it anew. |
29	| **Ashlar** | `mcm_` | A quoin that faces the operator — must be fair-faced (first-contact actionable), draws from the coffer (bounded operator vocabulary), registers on the project's broadside. Words in error output are ashlar. Definition-site marker: `axd_ashlar`. |
30	| **Hearting** | `mcm_` | Interior names — uncatalogued, prefix discipline only. Deliberate plainness so ashlar care stays affordable. |
31	| **Broadside** | `mcm_` | The project's single public sheet of ashlar vocabulary; registration there completes a mint. |
32	
33	### AXLA Vocabulary
34	
35	| Term | Prefix | Meaning |
36	|------|--------|---------|
37	| **Motif** | `axl_` | A reusable conceptual pattern from AXLA that a quoin can voice. The shared vocabulary across concept models. |
38	| **Voicing** | `axl_` | An annotation at a definition site declaring which AXLA motif a quoin instantiates (e.g., `//axk_premise`). |
39	| **Premise** | `axk_` | A motif representing a declared design constraint that bounds system complexity by stating what the system will not handle. |
40	| **Definition Site** | `axl_` | The location where a quoin's `[[anchor]]` and definition text appear. Where voicings are declared. |
41	
42	### Concept Model Patterns
43	
44	- **Linked Terms**: `{category_term}` — references defined vocabulary
45	- **Attribute References**: `:category_term: <<anchor,Display Text>>` — in mapping section
46	- **Anchors**: `[[anchor_name]]` — definition targets
47	- **Annotations**: `//axk_premise` — prefix-discriminated comment lines (no space after `//`, letter distinguishes from regular comments); primary motif first, `axd_` dimensions follow, voices relationship implicit. Legacy `//axl_voices …` form is read-only.
48	
49	### Minting Guidance
50	
51	Before introducing new quoin prefixes in an MCM document, consult the CLAUDE.md "Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline" subsection and minting memo Pattern J (`Memos/memo-20260110-acronym-selection-study.md`). The rule: uniform `prefixXY_word` shape, hard 2-letter ceiling, within-domain Y monosemy, documented sub-letter legend in the spec's mapping section.
52	
53	**Available commands:**
54	- `/cma-prep-pr` - Prepare upstream contribution
55

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about workforce identity federation audit logging and the fields you're asking about. Here's what I found:

## Key Audit Log Fields in Workforce Identity Federation

When a federated user accesses Google Cloud using Workforce Identity Federation, their identity appears in audit logs in the principalSubject field of authenticationInfo, which includes a structured identity string indicating the workforce pool and the external subject (usually an email or unique username).

## GenerateAccessToken and Impersonation

After mapping the external identity within Google Cloud, it can use the GenerateAccessToken method to impersonate a specific service account. The principalSubject field contains the subject of the IdP token—on AWS, this field contains the Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the principal; on Azure, this field contains the object ID of the managed identity; and for other OIDC IdPs, this field contains the value of the sub or subject claim from the OIDC token.

## DelegationInfo for Attribution

After the impersonation, any actions performed using the impersonated service account will appear in the logs as regular operations—but they can still be traced back to the original external identity through the serviceAccountDelegationInfo field. The impersonated GCP Service Account appears in the principalEmail, while the original Kubernetes identity is captured in the serviceAccountDelegationInfo.principalSubject field.

This delegation information provides crucial attribution, enabling you to trace back actions to their original external identity source even after service account impersonation occurs.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's relevant information about workforce identity federation with OIDC providers and handling non-publicly reachable issuers:

## Key Information

If your OIDC provider doesn't provide information or your issuer is not publicly accessible, you can manually upload the JWKs. This is a crucial feature for private identity providers.

### JWKs Upload for Private Issuers

JWK_JSON_PATH is an optional path to a locally uploaded OIDC JWKs. If this parameter isn't supplied, Google Cloud instead uses your IdP's /.well-known/openid-configuration path to source the JWKs containing the public keys.

### Important Limitations

There are specific restrictions on how locally uploaded JWKs can be used: Local OIDC JWKs can be uploaded through implicit flow or code flow, but can only be used in programmatic flow, in which you directly call the STS /token endpoint with a credential from the third-party IdP to exchange for a Google Cloud access token for your workforce pool. You can't use local OIDC JWKs when signing in to the console (federated).

### Configuration

In Issuer (URL), you must enter the issuer URI. The OIDC issuer URI must be in a valid URI format and start with https; for example, https://example.com/oidc.

This approach enables you to configure workforce identity federation with private OIDC providers that aren't publicly reachable by uploading the JSON Web Key Set directly.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
Keycloak item answered: **viable for exactly our hermetic case**. Workforce OIDC providers accept an uploaded `jwksJson` precisely for issuers that aren't publicly reachable — Google then never dials the issuer — with one documented restriction: uploaded JWKS work **only in the programmatic flow** (direct STS `/token` exchange, which is our Leg 2) and cannot serve console federated sign-in. A Keycloak-in-a-crucible with an `https://`-shaped issuer string and exported JWKS is therefore a legitimate standing test IdP. Sources: [manage workforce pool providers](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/manage-workforce-identity-pools-providers), [configure workforce identity federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation).

input.content:
# Federation Legs Spike — Findings (₢BZAAA)

Date: 2026-06-12

Status: Evidence record for the ₣BZ federation-legs spike. The three-leg
office-federation runtime chain is **proven end-to-end live** against the
canonical standing depot, in pure curl/jq (no gcloud at runtime). Setup
ceremony used payor-token REST (curl) throughout; the only console work was
Microsoft-side (Entra tenant + app registration).

---

## The proven chain (the headline)

A federated principal minted an office-SA token from a clean shell and called
a depot API with it:

1. **Leg 1 — Entra device flow (RFC 8628).** POST to
   `login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/oauth2/v2.0/devicecode`, human approved
   in browser, poll returned the OIDC ID token. Scope `openid profile email` —
   **no `offline_access`, and the response carried no refresh token**
   (D2 verified live: `has_refresh_token: false`).
2. **Leg 2 — STS exchange (RFC 8693).** POST `sts.googleapis.com/v1/token`,
   no auth header, audience = the provider resource name → Google federated
   access token, `expires_in 3598`. **No billing/quota project was required
   at this leg.**
3. **Leg 3 — `generateAccessToken`.** POST
   `iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/projects/-/serviceAccounts/{office-SA}:generateAccessToken`
   bearing the federated token **plus `x-goog-user-project: {depot}`**
   (see Finding F2) → office token, `expireTime` exactly +1 h.
4. **Bearer call.** `artifactregistry.googleapis.com/v1/.../repositories`
   listed the depot's GAR repo with the office token — **no quota-project
   header needed** (an impersonated office token is a plain SA token; the
   product support matrix and the quota-project ceremony are both dodged
   downstream, as the architecture premised).

Test fixture (standing test trust candidate, per the docket cinch):

- Org `scaleinvariant.org` = `organizations/247899326218`
- Pool `locations/global/workforcePools/spike-office-test`, session 3600s
- Provider `spike-entra`: issuer
  `https://login.microsoftonline.com/beb3e682-4589-4228-bcf9-27d48c125dbd/v2.0`,
  clientId `6d54c87a-e6c4-47d0-81e6-b91fe470ee1c`,
  mapping `google.subject = assertion.oid`, webSsoConfig ID_TOKEN /
  ONLY_ID_TOKEN_CLAIMS
- Office SA `spike-office-test@cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001.iam.gserviceaccount.com`
  with `roles/artifactregistry.reader` on the depot project
- Admission binding: `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on the office SA
  for `principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/spike-office-test/subject/9657166c-8a2d-4f5d-bcd1-ef481ee31f3e`
  (the Entra user's immutable `oid`) — at spike end this is the **only**
  binding on the SA policy, the one-binding admission shape working as cinched
- Entra side: free tenant `beb3e682-…`, app registration `spike-office-test`
  (single tenant, public client flows enabled, no secret, no redirect URI)

## Findings that feed the founding/admission ceremonies

- **F1 — Payor needs `roles/iam.workforcePoolAdmin` at org level.** The
  payor's standing org roles (`organizationAdmin`, `orgpolicy.policyAdmin`)
  do **not** include workforce-pool permissions; pool list/create 403'd until
  the payor self-granted it (`organizationAdmin` suffices to self-grant; the
  grant propagated in ~10 s). Establish-federation must seat this role.
- **F2 — Leg 3 requires a quota project; admission must grant
  `roles/serviceusage.serviceUsageConsumer`.** A workforce federated token
  carries no API-consumer project; bare `generateAccessToken` fails
  `403 PERMISSION_DENIED: "Method doesn't allow unregistered callers"` —
  a Palisade signature worth recognizing on sight (it reads like an API-key
  complaint, not an IAM denial, and no propagation wait fixes it). Fix:
  `x-goog-user-project: {depot}` header on Leg 3 + grant the federated
  principal `serviceUsageConsumer` on the depot. With both in place the call
  succeeded on the first attempt. `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` was already
  ENABLED on the depot (levy-time state, no action needed).
  Admission ceremony therefore writes **two** bindings, not one:
  tokenCreator on the office SA + serviceUsageConsumer on the depot project.
  The paddock's "admission is one binding" needs this amendment — flagged,
  not absorbed.
- **F3 — STS Leg 2 needs nothing extra.** No userProject, no auth header.
  The canon's Leg 2 description stands exactly as written.

## Verification items (docket "Done when" list)

- **V1 — Office-token lifetime ceiling: 1 h default and max; 12 h only via
  org policy.** Paper: the lifetime-extension constraint is
  `constraints/iam.allowServiceAccountCredentialLifetimeExtension` (list
  constraint naming the SA). Live: requesting `lifetime: 43200s` was refused
  `400 INVALID_ARGUMENT` with the constraint named in the error text. The
  retriever-office 12 h posture rides exactly this org-policy listing.
  Note the distinction: the workforce **session** duration (pool
  `sessionDuration`, 15 min–12 h) caps the federated token; the **office**
  token is independently capped at 1 h/12 h-by-policy. The two ceilings
  compose; an office token CAN outlive the workforce session that minted it.
- **V2 — AR / Cloud Build workforce federated-token support matrix:** both
  **GA, "No known limitations"** (Identity federation: products and
  limitations table). Adjacent wrinkle recorded: an AR troubleshooting note
  describes federated-user 400s on pushes/pulls tied to attribute mappings in
  the AR URL — moot under office impersonation but evidence for why the
  matrix-dodge premise earns its keep.
- **V3 — Audit-log shape under impersonation:**
  - `GenerateAccessToken` is audit-logged **always-on** — the depot had no
    `auditConfigs` at all, yet every mint produced a `data_access` entry.
    Corollary: `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` **rejects service-level audit
    config** (`400: does not … support service level configuration of Google
    Cloud audit logging`) because its token-mint logging is not optional.
  - **Surprise, flagged:** the always-on mint entry attributes
    `authenticationInfo.principalEmail` to the **office SA itself**, with a
    `loggableShortLivedCredential` token prefix — the federated caller's
    `principalSubject` is **absent** from the mint record, contradicting the
    documented workforce example-log shape and weakening the
    "every use is logged with subject" custody claim for the mint hop
    specifically. The caller IP and user agent are present.
  - Downstream attribution (`serviceAccountDelegationInfo` on the bearer
    call's DATA_READ entry): PENDING — AR DATA_READ audit logs were enabled
    mid-spike and the entry was still propagating at memo time; result lands
    below when observed.
- **V4 — STS egress-rule shape under VPC-SC (paper):** the workforce pool is
  org-level and cannot sit inside a perimeter, so STS calls whose audience is
  the pool need an **egress rule with `identityType: ANY_IDENTITY`** (the
  token endpoint is unauthenticated) to `sts.googleapis.com`, method `*`.
  `iamcredentials` is GA under VPC-SC with no known limitations; workforce
  principals are nameable in ingress/egress rules.
- **V5 — Keycloak-uploaded-JWKS viability (paper): viable for the hermetic
  test case.** Workforce OIDC providers accept `jwksJson` precisely for
  issuers that are not publicly reachable (Google then never dials the
  issuer; the issuer URI is a string match needing only `https://` shape).
  Documented restriction: uploaded JWKS serve **only the programmatic flow**
  (direct STS `/token` exchange — exactly Leg 2); console federated sign-in
  cannot use them. A Keycloak-in-a-crucible standing test IdP is therefore
  legitimate for suite personas; console access would need the real-IdP
  trust instead.

## Session-cache shape — elected (MVP)

What is cached: **the STS federated token only** (plus its expiry and the
subject), in one per-operator-session scratch file, 0600 in a 0700 dir,
tmpfs-preferred (`$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` on Linux, `$TMPDIR` on macOS), written
atomically (temp + rename). Never a regime, never `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`
(two-run clobber horizon), never `BURD_TEMP_DIR` (per-invocation lifetime is
too short — the cache must span tabtarget processes within one session).

- Office tokens are **not** cached: each verb-run mints its office token from
  the cached federated token (one cheap REST call, F2 header included) and
  holds it in process memory only. This keeps the higher-authority artifact
  ephemeral and makes one-office-per-token blast radius automatic.
- Accessor behavior: cache hit and unexpired → use; miss/expired and a TTY is
  present → run Leg 1 inline (print `verification_uri` + `user_code`),
  then Legs 2 and cache; miss/expired and **no TTY → fail loud** (the
  headless fail-fast membrane, canon D3/D2).
- The Entra ID token is never persisted at all — consumed by Leg 2 in-process.

## Incidental observations

- The depot's `artifactregistry.reader` binding carries three
  `deleted:serviceAccount:…?uid=` tombstones from prior retriever churn —
  the credential-churn memo's tombstone pattern observed live in the standing
  depot.
- Entra onboarding snag for solo evaluators: a bare personal Microsoft
  account cannot register apps ("outside of a directory has been
  deprecated"); the M365 Developer Program path is effectively closed to solo
  signups, and the working path is a free Azure signup (card required, $0)
  which provisions a real tenant. ~15 min of ceremony, one-time. Tenant and
  app registration themselves are free.
- `cloud.google.com` doc URLs 301 to `docs.cloud.google.com` and the docs
  pages truncate under naive fetch; the support-matrix table needed a raw
  HTML pull to read the AR/Cloud Build rows.

## Cloud-resource ledger (all additive, spike-created)

| Resource | Disposition |
|---|---|
| Org binding: payor → `iam.workforcePoolAdmin` | Keep (founding ceremony needs it) or revoke post-spike — operator call |
| Pool `spike-office-test` + provider `spike-entra` | Candidate standing test trust (docket cinch) |
| SA `spike-office-test@…` + its tokenCreator binding | Spike fixture; delete or keep with the pool |
| Depot bindings: SA → `artifactregistry.reader`, principal → `serviceUsageConsumer` | Spike fixtures; same disposition as the SA |
| Depot auditConfig: AR `DATA_READ` | Enabled mid-spike for V3; harmless to keep, trivial to drop |
| Entra: free tenant + `spike-office-test` app | Microsoft-side, operator-owned |

No existing depot SAs, bindings, or regimes were modified. The only
non-additive touch is the depot auditConfig row above.

toolUseResult.content:
# Federation Legs Spike — Findings (₢BZAAA)

Date: 2026-06-12

Status: Evidence record for the ₣BZ federation-legs spike. The three-leg
office-federation runtime chain is **proven end-to-end live** against the
canonical standing depot, in pure curl/jq (no gcloud at runtime). Setup
ceremony used payor-token REST (curl) throughout; the only console work was
Microsoft-side (Entra tenant + app registration).

---

## The proven chain (the headline)

A federated principal minted an office-SA token from a clean shell and called
a depot API with it:

1. **Leg 1 — Entra device flow (RFC 8628).** POST to
   `login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/oauth2/v2.0/devicecode`, human approved
   in browser, poll returned the OIDC ID token. Scope `openid profile email` —
   **no `offline_access`, and the response carried no refresh token**
   (D2 verified live: `has_refresh_token: false`).
2. **Leg 2 — STS exchange (RFC 8693).** POST `sts.googleapis.com/v1/token`,
   no auth header, audience = the provider resource name → Google federated
   access token, `expires_in 3598`. **No billing/quota project was required
   at this leg.**
3. **Leg 3 — `generateAccessToken`.** POST
   `iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/projects/-/serviceAccounts/{office-SA}:generateAccessToken`
   bearing the federated token **plus `x-goog-user-project: {depot}`**
   (see Finding F2) → office token, `expireTime` exactly +1 h.
4. **Bearer call.** `artifactregistry.googleapis.com/v1/.../repositories`
   listed the depot's GAR repo with the office token — **no quota-project
   header needed** (an impersonated office token is a plain SA token; the
   product support matrix and the quota-project ceremony are both dodged
   downstream, as the architecture premised).

Test fixture (standing test trust candidate, per the docket cinch):

- Org `scaleinvariant.org` = `organizations/247899326218`
- Pool `locations/global/workforcePools/spike-office-test`, session 3600s
- Provider `spike-entra`: issuer
  `https://login.microsoftonline.com/beb3e682-4589-4228-bcf9-27d48c125dbd/v2.0`,
  clientId `6d54c87a-e6c4-47d0-81e6-b91fe470ee1c`,
  mapping `google.subject = assertion.oid`, webSsoConfig ID_TOKEN /
  ONLY_ID_TOKEN_CLAIMS
- Office SA `spike-office-test@cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001.iam.gserviceaccount.com`
  with `roles/artifactregistry.reader` on the depot project
- Admission binding: `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on the office SA
  for `principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/spike-office-test/subject/9657166c-8a2d-4f5d-bcd1-ef481ee31f3e`
  (the Entra user's immutable `oid`) — at spike end this is the **only**
  binding on the SA policy, the one-binding admission shape working as cinched
- Entra side: free tenant `beb3e682-…`, app registration `spike-office-test`
  (single tenant, public client flows enabled, no secret, no redirect URI)

## Findings that feed the founding/admission ceremonies

- **F1 — Payor needs `roles/iam.workforcePoolAdmin` at org level.** The
  payor's standing org roles (`organizationAdmin`, `orgpolicy.policyAdmin`)
  do **not** include workforce-pool permissions; pool list/create 403'd until
  the payor self-granted it (`organizationAdmin` suffices to self-grant; the
  grant propagated in ~10 s). Establish-federation must seat this role.
- **F2 — Leg 3 requires a quota project; admission must grant
  `roles/serviceusage.serviceUsageConsumer`.** A workforce federated token
  carries no API-consumer project; bare `generateAccessToken` fails
  `403 PERMISSION_DENIED: "Method doesn't allow unregistered callers"` —
  a Palisade signature worth recognizing on sight (it reads like an API-key
  complaint, not an IAM denial, and no propagation wait fixes it). Fix:
  `x-goog-user-project: {depot}` header on Leg 3 + grant the federated
  principal `serviceUsageConsumer` on the depot. With both in place the call
  succeeded on the first attempt. `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` was already
  ENABLED on the depot (levy-time state, no action needed).
  Admission ceremony therefore writes **two** bindings, not one:
  tokenCreator on the office SA + serviceUsageConsumer on the depot project.
  The paddock's "admission is one binding" needs this amendment — flagged,
  not absorbed.
- **F3 — STS Leg 2 needs nothing extra.** No userProject, no auth header.
  The canon's Leg 2 description stands exactly as written.

## Verification items (docket "Done when" list)

- **V1 — Office-token lifetime ceiling: 1 h default and max; 12 h only via
  org policy.** Paper: the lifetime-extension constraint is
  `constraints/iam.allowServiceAccountCredentialLifetimeExtension` (list
  constraint naming the SA). Live: requesting `lifetime: 43200s` was refused
  `400 INVALID_ARGUMENT` with the constraint named in the error text. The
  retriever-office 12 h posture rides exactly this org-policy listing.
  Note the distinction: the workforce **session** duration (pool
  `sessionDuration`, 15 min–12 h) caps the federated token; the **office**
  token is independently capped at 1 h/12 h-by-policy. The two ceilings
  compose; an office token CAN outlive the workforce session that minted it.
- **V2 — AR / Cloud Build workforce federated-token support matrix:** both
  **GA, "No known limitations"** (Identity federation: products and
  limitations table). Adjacent wrinkle recorded: an AR troubleshooting note
  describes federated-user 400s on pushes/pulls tied to attribute mappings in
  the AR URL — moot under office impersonation but evidence for why the
  matrix-dodge premise earns its keep.
- **V3 — Audit-log shape under impersonation:**
  - `GenerateAccessToken` is audit-logged **always-on** — the depot had no
    `auditConfigs` at all, yet every mint produced a `data_access` entry.
    Corollary: `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` **rejects service-level audit
    config** (`400: does not … support service level configuration of Google
    Cloud audit logging`) because its token-mint logging is not optional.
  - **Surprise, flagged:** the always-on mint entry attributes
    `authenticationInfo.principalEmail` to the **office SA itself**, with a
    `loggableShortLivedCredential` token prefix — the federated caller's
    `principalSubject` is **absent** from the mint record, contradicting the
    documented workforce example-log shape and weakening the
    "every use is logged with subject" custody claim for the mint hop
    specifically. The caller IP and user agent are present.
  - Downstream attribution (`serviceAccountDelegationInfo` on the bearer
    call's DATA_READ entry): PENDING — AR DATA_READ audit logs were enabled
    mid-spike and the entry was still propagating at memo time; result lands
    below when observed.
- **V4 — STS egress-rule shape under VPC-SC (paper):** the workforce pool is
  org-level and cannot sit inside a perimeter, so STS calls whose audience is
  the pool need an **egress rule with `identityType: ANY_IDENTITY`** (the
  token endpoint is unauthenticated) to `sts.googleapis.com`, method `*`.
  `iamcredentials` is GA under VPC-SC with no known limitations; workforce
  principals are nameable in ingress/egress rules.
- **V5 — Keycloak-uploaded-JWKS viability (paper): viable for the hermetic
  test case.** Workforce OIDC providers accept `jwksJson` precisely for
  issuers that are not publicly reachable (Google then never dials the
  issuer; the issuer URI is a string match needing only `https://` shape).
  Documented restriction: uploaded JWKS serve **only the programmatic flow**
  (direct STS `/token` exchange — exactly Leg 2); console federated sign-in
  cannot use them. A Keycloak-in-a-crucible standing test IdP is therefore
  legitimate for suite personas; console access would need the real-IdP
  trust instead.

## Session-cache shape — elected (MVP)

What is cached: **the STS federated token only** (plus its expiry and the
subject), in one per-operator-session scratch file, 0600 in a 0700 dir,
tmpfs-preferred (`$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` on Linux, `$TMPDIR` on macOS), written
atomically (temp + rename). Never a regime, never `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`
(two-run clobber horizon), never `BURD_TEMP_DIR` (per-invocation lifetime is
too short — the cache must span tabtarget processes within one session).

- Office tokens are **not** cached: each verb-run mints its office token from
  the cached federated token (one cheap REST call, F2 header included) and
  holds it in process memory only. This keeps the higher-authority artifact
  ephemeral and makes one-office-per-token blast radius automatic.
- Accessor behavior: cache hit and unexpired → use; miss/expired and a TTY is
  present → run Leg 1 inline (print `verification_uri` + `user_code`),
  then Legs 2 and cache; miss/expired and **no TTY → fail loud** (the
  headless fail-fast membrane, canon D3/D2).
- The Entra ID token is never persisted at all — consumed by Leg 2 in-process.

## Incidental observations

- The depot's `artifactregistry.reader` binding carries three
  `deleted:serviceAccount:…?uid=` tombstones from prior retriever churn —
  the credential-churn memo's tombstone pattern observed live in the standing
  depot.
- Entra onboarding snag for solo evaluators: a bare personal Microsoft
  account cannot register apps ("outside of a directory has been
  deprecated"); the M365 Developer Program path is effectively closed to solo
  signups, and the working path is a free Azure signup (card required, $0)
  which provisions a real tenant. ~15 min of ceremony, one-time. Tenant and
  app registration themselves are free.
- `cloud.google.com` doc URLs 301 to `docs.cloud.google.com` and the docs
  pages truncate under naive fetch; the support-matrix table needed a raw
  HTML pull to read the AR/Cloud Build rows.

## Cloud-resource ledger (all additive, spike-created)

| Resource | Disposition |
|---|---|
| Org binding: payor → `iam.workforcePoolAdmin` | Keep (founding ceremony needs it) or revoke post-spike — operator call |
| Pool `spike-office-test` + provider `spike-entra` | Candidate standing test trust (docket cinch) |
| SA `spike-office-test@…` + its tokenCreator binding | Spike fixture; delete or keep with the pool |
| Depot bindings: SA → `artifactregistry.reader`, principal → `serviceUsageConsumer` | Spike fixtures; same disposition as the SA |
| Depot auditConfig: AR `DATA_READ` | Enabled mid-spike for V3; harmless to keep, trivial to drop |
| Entra: free tenant + `spike-office-test` app | Microsoft-side, operator-owned |

No existing depot SAs, bindings, or regimes were modified. The only
non-additive touch is the depot auditConfig row above.

Memo drafted at `Memos/memo-20260612-federation-legs-spike-findings.md` — everything is in except the downstream `serviceAccountDelegationInfo` observation (V3), which the background AR-audit query will settle in a few minutes. Two flags are recorded rather than absorbed, per the docket:

1. **Admission is two bindings, not one** — the paddock's office-architecture section says admission is a single tokenCreator binding, but Leg 3 also demands `serviceUsageConsumer` on the depot for the quota project.
2. **The mint audit record omits the federated caller** — always-on `GenerateAccessToken` logging attributes only the office SA + token prefix, no `principalSubject`.

When the query returns I'll finalize V3 and report the full spike picture.

aiTitle: Find heat source for uncommitted memos

input.old_string:
- Downstream attribution (`serviceAccountDelegationInfo` on the bearer
    call's DATA_READ entry): PENDING — AR DATA_READ audit logs were enabled
    mid-spike and the entry was still propagating at memo time; result lands
    below when observed.
input.new_string:
- **Downstream attribution observed, fully intact.** With AR audit logs
    enabled, the office-token `ListRepositories` call logged:
    `principalEmail` = the office SA, and
    `serviceAccountDelegationInfo[].principalSubject` =
    `principal://…/workforcePools/spike-office-test/subject/{oid}` — the
    human, by immutable IdP claim. Every office-token use is attributable to
    the federate at the using service's log. Two conditions for that trail:
    (a) the service's audit logs must be enabled on the depot (opt-in,

    DATA_READ-only config produced nothing for it. Artifact pulls are
    DATA_READ; enable both for retriever-trail coverage.
  - Net audit posture: the mint hop names the SA + caller IP but not the
    human; the use hop names both SA and human. Attribution lives downstream.

content.text:
**₢BZAAA is done-when-complete.** Findings memo notched at `a7993186a` (`Memos/memo-20260612-federation-legs-spike-findings.md`).

**The headline:** a federated human — your Entra `oid` — minted an office-SA token from a clean shell in pure curl/jq and listed the standing depot's GAR repository with it. Device flow returned no refresh token (D2 verified live), STS needed nothing beyond the canon's description, and the office token needed no quota-project header downstream (the matrix-dodge premise, proven).

**All five verification items answered**, two with live evidence beyond the paper ask:

- Lifetime: 1 h hard ceiling, 12 h only via the `allowServiceAccountCredentialLifetimeExtension` org policy — refusal observed live with the constraint named in the error.
- Audit: mint hop logs SA + caller IP (always-on, can't be configured); use hop logs `serviceAccountDelegationInfo` naming the human — observed live, but only after enabling AR audit logs and discovering `ListRepositories` is ADMIN_READ.
- AR and Cloud Build: both GA, no known limitations. VPC-SC: STS needs an `ANY_IDENTITY` egress rule. Keycloak: viable hermetically via uploaded JWKS, programmatic flow only.

**Two flags against the paddock's office-architecture section** (flagged, not absorbed):
1. **Admission is two bindings, not one** — Leg 3 requires `serviceUsageConsumer` on the depot for the federated principal, alongside tokenCreator on the office SA.
2. **The mint audit record omits the federated caller** — attribution is real but lives downstream, and only where the target service's audit logs are enabled (a levy-ceremony decision).

**Session-cache shape elected** (in the memo): cache only the STS federated token per-session (tmpfs-preferred, 0600, atomic write); mint office tokens per verb-run, in-process only; no TTY + no live session → fail loud.

Open dispositions for you, recorded in the memo's resource ledger: keep or revoke the payor's `workforcePoolAdmin` org grant, and whether `spike-office-test` pool/provider/SA stays as the standing test trust.

Ready to wrap ₢BZAAA?

input.command: jjx_record
input.params: {"identity": "BZAAA", "files": ["Memos/memo-20260612-federation-legs-spike-findings.md"], "intent": "Findings memo gains a Why-this-spike section: the conversion bet the single-tier model on a never-executed runtime chain, and this spike is the ordering's gate for the verb movement — wobbles surface at throwaway-script cost before the civic verbs and accessor branch are slated"}

content.text: e222cea7bf1093fad2fd8e3ae02ccde7b638e0f5

Notched at `e222cea7b`. Here are the surprises from the spike, weightiest first:

**1. "Admission is one binding" didn't survive contact.** The paddock's cleanest claim — admit an operator with a single tokenCreator binding — turns out to need a second binding. A workforce federated token carries no quota project, so `generateAccessToken` rejects it with a misleading `403: "Method doesn't allow unregistered callers"` (it reads like an API-key complaint, not IAM, and no propagation wait helps). The fix is an `x-goog-user-project` header on Leg 3 **plus** `serviceUsageConsumer` for the principal on the depot. Flagged in the memo for the paddock's office-architecture section.

**2. The mint audit record doesn't name the human.** Google always-on-logs every `GenerateAccessToken` (you can't even configure it — the API rejects service-level audit config), but that entry attributes only the office SA and caller IP. The federated caller's `principalSubject` is absent. The human *is* fully named downstream — every office-token call carries `serviceAccountDelegationInfo` with the `principal://…/subject/{oid}` — but only where the target service's audit logs are switched on, which they aren't by default. So "every use is logged with subject" becomes a levy-ceremony obligation, not a free property. Also: `ListRepositories` logs as ADMIN_READ, not DATA_READ — easy to enable the wrong class and see nothing.

**3. The payor can't touch workforce pools out of the box.** `organizationAdmin` doesn't include workforce-pool permissions; the payor had to self-grant `iam.workforcePoolAdmin` at org level. Establish-federation must seat this role explicitly.

**4. Microsoft made the on-ramp worse than the docket assumed.** A bare personal Microsoft account can no longer register apps, and the M365 Developer Program is effectively closed to solo signups — the working path was a free Azure signup (card required, $0 charged). That's real friction for the solo-evaluator founding hour and is recorded for the onboarding story.

**5. A self-inflicted one, kept for the lesson:** my spike queries piped REST responses through `jq` field-extractors, which silently turned expired-payor-token 401s into `null`/`[]` — half an hour chasing a "vanished" audit config and "missing" log entries that were really auth failures. The memo records the discipline: branch on `.error` before extracting.

On the good-news side, the things the architecture *bet on* all held: no refresh token exists anywhere (verified live), STS Leg 2 needs nothing extra, the office token dodges both the product matrix and the quota-project ceremony downstream, and the 12 h lifetime refusal named exactly the org-policy knob the retriever posture plans to use.

Ready to wrap ₢BZAAA whenever you say.
